title: Capacity plan for 3 concurrent projects sharing a 20-person team
description: A 20-person cross-functional team (6 backend, 5 frontend, 4 QA, 2 DevOps, 2 designers, 1 architect) must deliver 3 concurrent projects over the next 2 quarters. Project Alpha requires 60% backend-heavy effort with a hard go-live in 8 weeks. Project Beta is frontend-heavy at 50% with a soft deadline in 12 weeks. Project Gamma is a greenfield initiative requiring all skill types equally over 16 weeks. The team currently operates at 82% average utilization. Produce a capacity plan that allocates resources across all three projects, identifies skill-type bottlenecks, and recommends adjustments to prevent over-allocation.
expected_behaviors: Demand forecast disaggregated by skill type (backend, frontend, QA, DevOps, design) per project per sprint
Supply model reflecting 20 team members with role-specific net availability after overhead
Identification of backend bottleneck during Alpha's peak weeks overlapping with Gamma ramp-up
Gap analysis showing frontend surplus during Alpha-heavy sprints and deficit during Beta peak
Utilization projections per person staying within 70-85% sustainable pace zone
Recommendations for staggering project phases or temporarily shifting resources between projects
Risk flags for the architect as single point of failure across all three projects

title: Capacity gap when 2 key developers resign mid-project
description: A delivery team of 12 is 60% through a critical migration project with a contractual go-live date in 10 weeks. The lead backend architect (sole owner of the legacy integration layer) and a senior full-stack developer (leading the new API design) both submit resignations with 2-week notice periods. The remaining team has partial knowledge of their domains but no one can independently own either workstream. Current velocity is 42 story points per sprint; the remaining backlog is 210 points. Assess the capacity impact, model recovery scenarios, and recommend a remediation plan.
expected_behaviors: Supply model updated to reflect loss of 2 key resources with knowledge concentration risk
Velocity impact analysis projecting drop from 42 to approximately 28-32 points per sprint
Gap analysis showing the 210-point backlog is undeliverable in 10 weeks with reduced capacity
Knowledge transfer plan maximizing the 2-week notice period for critical domain handoff
Remediation options scored: emergency contractor hire, scope negotiation, deadline extension, team overtime (flagged as unsustainable)
Utilization optimizer flagging remaining team members at risk of exceeding 90% if absorbing extra load
Recommendation for composite approach: partial scope deferral + 1 contractor + structured knowledge transfer

title: Seasonal capacity adjustment for a team with 30% PTO in December
description: A 15-person product development team has a release scheduled for January 15. Historical data shows that December averages 30% PTO across the team, with the last two weeks of December seeing only 40% of normal staffing. Three team members have already requested the entire last two weeks off; two more plan a full week each. The current sprint velocity is 35 points, and 85 points of work remain including 25 points of critical-path items that must be completed before the holiday period to avoid blocking the January release. Model the seasonal capacity reduction and recommend a plan to protect the January release date.
expected_behaviors: Supply model with weekly granularity showing December capacity declining from ~12 effective FTEs to ~6 FTEs in the last two weeks
Demand forecast front-loading critical-path items into early December sprints
Gap analysis identifying that 85 points at reduced velocity (~20 points/sprint in December) requires 4+ sprints but only 2 remain
Recommendation to pull critical-path work (25 points) into November's last sprint or first December sprint
Utilization optimizer ensuring early-December sprints don't push available staff above 90%
Contingency plan for January first week with skeleton crew handling any December carryover
Risk flag for integration testing dependencies that require multiple team members simultaneously

title: Capacity planning for a SAFe program with 5 teams across 4 PIs
description: A SAFe program consists of 5 Agile Release Trains (ARTs) with teams of 8-10 members each (total 45 people). The program is planning across 4 Program Increments (PIs), each 10 weeks (5 sprints of 2 weeks). PI-1 features are committed; PI-2 features are planned with 80% confidence; PI-3 and PI-4 are roadmap items at 50% and 30% confidence respectively. Two teams share a common platform dependency. One team has 3 members rolling off after PI-2 due to contract end. The program targets an average load factor of 0.8 and innovation sprint allocation of 1 sprint per PI. Produce a program-level capacity plan across all 4 PIs with confidence-weighted demand and inter-team dependency management.
expected_behaviors: Demand forecast with confidence weighting: PI-1 at 100%, PI-2 at 80%, PI-3 at 50%, PI-4 at 30%
Supply model for 45 people across 5 teams with load factor 0.8 and 1 innovation sprint reserved per PI
Effective capacity per PI calculated as: team_size * sprints_available * load_factor * velocity_factor
Identification of platform team as bottleneck due to 2-team dependency creating contention
Gap analysis for PI-3 onwards reflecting contract staff departure reducing one team from 10 to 7
Recommendation for PI planning to over-allocate PI-1/PI-2 capacity and create buffer for PI-3/PI-4 uncertainty
Cross-team utilization analysis ensuring no single team consistently above 85% while others sit below 70%
Program-level capacity roadmap visualization showing confidence bands widening across PIs
